contribution from this region because it is area one, and area
has numbers which are very large, and this
one, because it is the number of rooms X2,
it is a small
number. So even if the area stays the
same (3156) and for one house the number of
rooms were three, and then for a
different house with the same area,
the number of rooms was, let's say, nine,
we obviously think that the
house with nine rooms should cost way
more than the house with
three rooms. But because the numbers are so small, the
difference in the price would not be
very high. Because of this number, because of the scale of it, even though
we think it should be high, it's not high.
In other words, the algorithm is paying more
attention to this feature than that
feature. It's thinking that this one is
something more important than that one.
Why? Because the number is high, and it's
just a mathematical formula. So we cannot
just directly take these features and
feed them to the algorithm. We first have to normalize their scale
in order to bring them into a common
scale. Not change the values of them,
of course, but change the scale of it so
that they vary on the same scale. This is
called data normalization. There are two
ways you can do that. One is the term
that is given to it, which is called data
normalization, and the second one is
called standardization.
Standardization: let's look at the standardization one.
This is one we'll be using, even though you can try that one as well.

So what it does is it takes the value in the column—in this case, since we're talking about the area column, which is X1—so it's going to the X1 column, pick values in it, and modify them in this way.
Let's pick a value: X11, where this top one represents the first example in the data.
You can think of it as this one.
What it is doing is subtracting from it the mean of the column—so mean represented by μ of X1—and then it is dividing it by the standard deviation represented by σ of X1.
It is doing this for the entire entries of that column, so it's not just doing it for X11; it's doing it for whatever number of values you have.
The result of it is you'll see that instead of the values varying between these high numbers, they then become values varying between some small negative number and some small positive number.

The relation stays the same—so remember, we are not changing the data; you cannot interfere with the data, but you have reduced its scale so that now they can vary between a small negative number and a small positive number.
You do this for the area, and you also do this for the number of rooms as well and whatever other features you might have—you do this for all of them so that your scale remains the same.
When you are doing linear regression on it, it doesn't bias towards a particular feature more than any other feature.
